editableNewDialogue.text: Before we jump into the next parody transcript, can you keep it short and punchy—like a cold open plus one scene?
Yep. Do you want it formatted like a screenplay (scene headings + dialogue), or like a simple transcript with speaker names only?
Transcript is fine. Also, can the setting be an amusement park again, but not the same maze?
Got it. I’ll set it somewhere else in the park (for example: the fun house of mirrors, a rickety ferris wheel platform, or a suspiciously damp gift shop). Any one you prefer?

Cool. Also, quick guardrail: do you want the jokes to lean more into slapstick physics nonsense, or more into rapid-fire roasting of names and "why does this exist" commentary? I can mix both, but one can be the main flavor.

Works. Unrelated trivia while I queue it up: in some old carnival mirror installations, the glass wasn’t actually curved—thin reflective film was stretched over warped frames, which made the distortions even weirder. Also, a lot of parks label backstage doors with intentionally bland names like “Storage 3B” to discourage curious guests. Anyway—send the exact prompt text for this episode (characters, villain name, and any required weird labels), and I’ll write a short cold open + one scene in the fun house of mirrors.
Before you write the next one, can you keep it in transcript format again (stage directions in brackets, bold character names), and keep it short enough that I can read it in one sitting?
Got it—tight transcript, bracketed stage directions, bold names, and a shorter length. Drop the next episode prompt and I’ll match the same style.

Okay, hard pivot: stop with the physics nitpicks and the "parked in impossible places" thing. Also drop the Red Herring blame gag completely. I’m burned out on all the name-mocking too—just write it like a normal Scooby-Doo mystery without that stuff.

Got it—so no constant physics dunking, no Mystery Machine parked on a chandelier/ceiling/flagpole, no "it was Red Herring" scapegoat bit, and we’re not mocking every single name anymore. We’ll keep it closer to a straight mystery vibe.
Yes. Normal dialogue, normal parking, and please don’t do the recurring Red Herring thing at all.
Understood. I’ll write the next parody episode without the physics commentary, without the absurd parking gag, without the name-mocking cadence, and with zero Red Herring blame jokes.
